description: Cross-domain dependency edges for Domains I-III (Chapters 1-10)

edges.description: SHM position is described by sine/cosine functions
SHM uses cosine for position from maximum displacement
Amplitude determines maximum displacement of SHM
Wave function is built on the sine function
Amplitude scales the wave function
Phase determines initial offset of the wave
Angular frequency is measured in radians per second
Phasors are complex numbers representing sinusoidal signals
Phasor representation is based on Euler's formula
Harmonics are indexed by natural number multiples
Musical intervals are rational number ratios
Sum-to-product identities derive from addition formulas related to double-angle identities
Sum-to-product formulas involve sine
Sum-to-product formulas involve cosine
Period generalizes periodicity from trigonometric functions
Mode amplitudes are found by inner product projection
Fourier series uses sine as basis functions
Fourier series uses cosine as basis functions
Fourier series is an orthogonal decomposition in the space of periodic functions
Type of interference depends on phase relationship
Limits are defined on the real numbers
Epsilon-delta definition uses absolute value for distance
Completeness ensures limits of convergent sequences exist in R
Deriving the derivative of sine
Deriving the derivative of cosine
Arc length formula uses the Pythagorean theorem at infinitesimal scale
Riemann sums operate on real-valued functions
The harmonic oscillator ODE is the mathematical formulation of SHM
The wave equation involves second partial derivatives
Group velocity is defined as d(omega)/dk, a derivative
The harmonic series sum is indexed over natural numbers
Integral test connects improper integrals to series like the harmonic series
Comparing Maclaurin series of e^(ix), cos(x), and i*sin(x) proves Euler's formula
Fourier coefficients are computed via definite integrals
Laplace transform is defined as an improper integral
IVT depends on the completeness of the real numbers (no gaps)

compositionRules.yields: Distance rate of change — computing how fast distance changes between moving objects
conditions: When measuring rate of change of distance between moving points
compositionRules.example: Ship A moves north at 3 km/h, ship B east at 4 km/h. Rate of distance change = d/dt sqrt(a^2 + b^2)

compositionRules.yields: Polar/phasor representation of waves — expressing waves as rotating vectors on the unit circle
conditions: When representing sinusoidal waves using complex exponential or rotating phasor
compositionRules.example: Wave A*sin(omega*t) = Im(A*e^(i*omega*t)), a rotating phasor on the unit circle scaled by A

compositionRules.yields: Average value of periodic functions — integrating sine over one period gives zero, half-period gives 2A/pi
conditions: When computing the average or RMS value of a sinusoidal signal

compositionRules.yields: Directional derivative — the rate of change of a function in the direction of a given vector
conditions: When computing how fast a multivariable function changes in a specific direction
compositionRules.example: D_u f = grad(f) . u = (df/dx, df/dy) . (u_1, u_2), the dot product of gradient and direction

compositionRules.yields: Standing wave mode solutions — separating space and time to find normal mode frequencies
conditions: When solving the wave equation on a bounded domain with boundary conditions
compositionRules.example: String fixed at both ends: u(x,t) = sin(n*pi*x/L)*cos(n*pi*c*t/L)

compositionRules.yields: Power series representations of wave-like functions — connecting harmonic decomposition to polynomial approximation
conditions: When representing periodic phenomena using both Fourier and Taylor approaches
compositionRules.example: sin(x) = x - x^3/6 + x^5/120 - ... (Taylor) vs sin(x) as the first Fourier basis function

compositionRules.yields: Instantaneous frequency — the derivative of the phase function gives the instantaneous frequency of a modulated signal
conditions: When a signal's frequency changes over time (frequency modulation)

compositionRules.yields: Fourier coefficient calculation via integration — FTC enables exact computation of Fourier coefficients
conditions: When computing Fourier coefficients of a known periodic function

compositionRules.yields: Integral superposition of continuous wave sources — replacing discrete sum with integral over a continuum
conditions: When summing contributions from a continuous distribution of wave sources
compositionRules.example: Diffraction integral: summing wavelets from each point of an aperture

compositionRules.yields: Foundation for epsilon-delta analysis — completeness guarantees that convergent Cauchy sequences have limits in R
conditions: When proving existence of limits using the completeness of the real numbers
compositionRules.example: The sequence 3, 3.1, 3.14, 3.141, 3.1415, ... converges to pi because R is complete
